trip.name: Budapest

currency.note: Hungary uses the forint, not the euro. Cards are widely accepted; keep some cash for markets and transit.

trip.start: A day or two to explore — flex it to taste. Base in the Palace Quarter, keep the riverside cool escape in hand if it turns hot.

do.hook: Palace reading rooms for a tiny fee — the midday heat refuge, and an only-in-Budapest sight.
Tree-shaded riverbank given over to local recreation — the centrepiece cool escape for the free day.
Shaded courtyard anchoring the base neighbourhood: work by day, jazz and creatives by night.
Book-lined Buda café — good for a work session or chatting with locals over a cheap lunch.
Gritty local alternative to the tourist ruin bars — live music and community murals.
Deeply shaded urban lake park — a heat-smart green break in Buda.
River-pebble deckchairs and a cheap wine spritzer among young locals; pairs with Római-part.
A preserved cobbled old-town square the tour groups skip — a calm evening stroll.
Forest ridge and a panorama tower a few degrees cooler than the city — the free hot-day escape, locals hiking and picnicking. The answer to the 31°C day.
Scenic climb into the Buda hills (line 60) — covered by your BKK travelcard, the shaded way up toward Normafa.
Monumental garden-cemetery and arboretum minutes from your base — free, deep shade, deeply local, no tour groups. The easy midday heat refuge.
Green shaded hillside where an old quarter once stood — free, calm, and the cool way up toward Gellért Hill.
The one iconic panorama worth the crowd — free. Climb at sunset via the shaded Tabán side to dodge the midday sun and the tour buses.
Creative-Buda strip — small galleries, design shops and student bars to duck into. Free to stroll, and your rain hedge for a showery afternoon.
Car-free green island where the whole city comes to run and cool off — free, shaded loop paths, local life. Best early morning or evening.
The oldest Ottoman thermal bath in the city — atmospheric, cheap, and far quieter than the tourist baths. A low-cost cool-off on the hot day.

trip.stayPick: Palace Quarter / Józsefváros (District VIII). The best blend — local texture, good work cafés, and the off-beat social-hostel scene, without dropping you in the District VII party core. Walkable to the centre, one tram from Buda.

hoods.d: Faded-grand intellectual streets, courtyards, soft alternative culture. Good café density, quieter than party Pest.
Creative Buda boulevard — students, galleries, low-key bars. Strong cafés, buzzy by day.
Leafy, calm, river-near; everyday Budapest, no crowds. Strong and calm for work.
Old-town square plus a tree-shaded riverbank — the cool-air escape. A half-day out, not a work zone.

eat.hook: Laptop-friendly, cheap modern Hungarian daily lunch.
A genuinely local market hall — ultra-budget street food, no tour groups.
The authentic garlic lángos locals defend — not the Retro Lángos tourist version.
Old-school diner — big traditional stews at true neighbourhood prices.
Ultra-budget fried hake by the river — eat it in the breeze.

avoid.hook: The lángos 'hidden topping' scam pushes a basic plate to €15–20. Use Rákóczi market instead.
The original ruin bar, now stag-party tourism with inflated prices; detached from local life.
Landmark interior, but hours-long lines and €10+ mediocre coffee; no locals.
Predatory margins up to 20%, hidden under '0% commission' signs.
Commercialised assembly-line lángos at a premium — the Lehel market stand is the real thing.
Street touts, hidden service charges, microwaved food along the whole strip.

mobility.verdict: BKK public transit — two consecutive 24-hour travelcards.
mobility.note: Covers trams, metro, buses and the H5 HÉV straight to Óbuda and Római-part. The heat-smart choice — when the afternoon spikes you hop on an air-conditioned tram instead of sweating it out. Bubi bikeshare is near-free, but a 45-minute ride to Óbuda in dead heat fails the heat filter.

timing.verdict: Acceptable. Peak summer, but ~27°C sits below the heat ceiling.
timing.note: No festival, strike or holiday flagged in this window — worth a quick re-check closer to the date. A good short opener, with the cool riverside day in hand if it turns hot.

trip.name: Belgrade

currency.note: Serbia uses the dinar. Cash-first culture — cards work in cafés but carry dinars for markets, kiosks and buses.

trip.start: Swim Ada Ciganlija and save the city walking for morning and evening — keep the worst afternoon hours on the water or under the trees.

do.hook: The cleaner, quieter local swimming stretch of the lake — the locals' side, not the crowded one.
A small boat to a car-free forested river island — wild swimming and shade, away from the crowds.
Top local work café — creative crowd, strong WiFi. The reliable midday session.
Repurposed Danube grain silos with open-air art and sunsets — the city's gritty post-industrial cool.
Bohemian tavern hidden deep in the island forest — a total midday heat escape, dirt-cheap.
A full free day of deep-shade forest walking — active without the sun.
The meet-people spot: a 25–35 backpacker and local crowd over cheap drinks, non-touristy.
Ultra-budget sunset beer on a local river barge.
Breezy hilltop over the Danube–Sava meeting point; free, and an evening sunset here beats the afternoon heat. Cluster it with the two chapels below.

do.nm: Church of Saint Sava (Hram Svetog Save)
do.hook: One of the largest Orthodox churches in the world; go down to the gold-mosaic crypt. Indoor — a good midday heat refuge.
Holds Tesla's ashes and live demos; English tours run on a schedule, so check times and expect a queue. Indoor.
A tiny atmospheric church (chandeliers made from WWI shell casings) and a holy-spring chapel, both inside the fortress walls.

do.nm: Museum of Yugoslavia (House of Flowers / Tito's tomb)
do.hook: Tito-era collection and mausoleum; less touristy, leafy grounds, indoor halls.
A central pocket of deep shade and a glasshouse — strong heat-escape between sights.

do.nm: Topčider Park (+ Residence of Prince Miloš)
do.hook: Big shaded park for a picnic plus a small historic royal residence — an easy green half-day.
Climb the old tower for the Zemun-side view, then the swan promenade below. Pairs with your Zemun pick.
Panoramic tower in cooler forested hills ~16 km south; a half-day trip out, best as a morning or late-afternoon run.
Cold-War jets in the famous dome hangar; indoor, out near the airport — pair it with arrival or departure. (Interpreted from 'Musee de lavation' — tell me if you meant something else.)

do.nm: Eternal Derby — Partizan vs Crvena zvezda
do.hook: Football or basketball, this is the local experience: raucous, non-touristy, evening. Late June is off-season, so a true derby likely won't fall in your window — check the fixture calendar for a friendly or other match.
The cobbled bohemian quarter — great for an evening stroll. It's the touristy old-town strip (your Tri šešira trap sits here), so come for the lanes and music, not a value dinner.
The central pedestrian promenade past the National Museum and National Theatre — the main artery, busy and central rather than hidden.
Modern luxury riverside development; pleasant for a flat evening walk, but it's the polished gentrified zone, not local soul.

trip.stayPick: Donji Dorćol, Stari Grad. The clear base — social-hostel scene, the densest cafés for light work, and the Danube on its doorstep to cool off, without putting you in the tourist core.

hoods.d: Gritty post-industrial cool with a hipster edge — dense specialty cafés, Danube nearby. Strong WiFi, buzzy but workable.
Green riverfront of local barge cafés — breezy, social, no tourists. More hangout than work.
Austro-Hungarian riverside village — narrow streets, fiercely local, off the cruise loop.
Leafy, hilly gateway to Košutnjak forest and the Ada Ciganlija beach — the cooling zone.

eat.hook: A legendary street window for a big cheap pljeskavica with onions and kajmak.
Air-conditioned upstairs work space, good WiFi — a cool refuge.
Local green market for ultra-cheap fruit, cheese and cured meats.
The famous traditional bakery — cheap burek with yogurt for breakfast.
Old-school working-class kafana — heavy traditional dishes, loud and real.
A ramshackle fishermen's club — fresh river fish and cheap beer, zero frills.
A food-focused river barge (not a party boat) — traditional food on the water with a breeze.
Local institution, massive cheap Serbian portions — your forest-day dinner.
Modern, refined Balkan cooking — a calm dinner away from the crowds.
Central green market for fruit, burek and pljeskavica to-go — an alternative to Bajloni.
Seafood — a solid sit-down fish dinner.
Relaxed courtyard terrace — good for a long dinner outdoors.
Hip hotel rooftop for lunch with a view over the oldest large-scale mural; pricier — go for the view.
The cult alternative floating club on the Sava — late-night party on the river, not a tourist splav.
Friend rec for live music + terrace. ⚠ Also on your tourist-trap list (overpriced, folklore show) — go for the music and atmosphere, not a value meal.
Friend rec for a seafood lunch. ⚠ Also on your tourist-trap list (good fish but pricey/upscale-tourist) — Radecki nearby is the cheaper local alternative.

trip.doNote2: Dishes to try (friend rec): pljeskavica (the big Serbian burger-patty — Grill 'Loki'); Karađorđeva šnicla (rolled breaded veal/pork cutlet stuffed with kajmak — interpreted from 'Karadjordjeva palacinka'); šopska salad (tomato, cucumber, onion, grated white cheese — interpreted from 'Shops salt'); burek (flaky breakfast pastry — Pekara Petrović).

avoid.hook: Overpriced kafana, mostly tourists paying a premium for a forced folklore show. (Also a friend rec — kept in the Eat list for the music + terrace; just don't expect value.)
See the historic building over a coffee, but locals say never eat there — tiny overpriced portions.
About €27 for a basic mixed-meat plate that costs half that in normal neighbourhoods.
Gentrified extraction zone for foreigners (Ambar, Toro Latin) — polished, overpriced, no local soul.
Good fish but overpriced upscale-tourist — use Radecki for the real fishermen's vibe. (Also a friend rec — kept in the Eat list; your call between the two.)
Unlicensed drivers charging 3–5×; use the fixed-price voucher desk or a ride app.

mobility.verdict: GSP public transport — buses, trams and trolleys, reportedly fare-free since ~2025.
mobility.cost: ~€0 if fare-free, else €1–2/ride + a few € for app-taxis
mobility.note: Buy a ticket until you confirm the free fares on the ground, so you don't risk a fine. Walk compact Dorćol and the centre; use transit to beat the heat to Zemun and Savski kej. For Ada Ciganlija and Košutnjak in peak afternoon, a CarGo or Yandex taxi is a few euros. Avoid unmarked airport taxis — voucher desk or app only.

timing.verdict: Acceptable, with a heat caveat — late June can break 30°C.
timing.note: That's why the plan is built around rivers, the island beach and the forest. Treat afternoons as swim-and-shade time; save the city walking for morning and evening.

trip.name: Prizren

currency.note: Kosovo uses the euro. Very cash-based outside the centre — carry small notes; ATMs are in town.

trip.start: Give the Sharr Mountains a full day — the signature mountain-air experience of the trip — and keep the town to the cooler dawn and dusk hours.

do.hook: The signature day — a cheap morning minibus up, a full-day moderate hike through pastures to a glacial lake, and a cold swim in ~20°C air.
Climb the silent cobbled Nënkalaja alleys at 6am to beat the heat and watch the city wake.
A black tea on a low stool with older locals — the most unfiltered local routine in town.
A locals-saved open-air cinema, now a shaded cultural café — good for a cool two-hour work stint.

workMeta.longstay: “good for a cool two-hour work stint”

do.hook: Duck inside the 16th-century bathhouse's thick stone walls for art and a total midday heat escape.
A flat, deeply shaded riverside forest walk — the low-effort refuge when the town is baking.
Unpretentious bar where local bohemians and backpackers mix — the anti-tourist evening spot.

trip.doNote: Mountain-day alternatives: a longer 14km cultural pasture hike to Gornje Lubinje, or a detour from Prevallë to the abandoned Yugoslav ski resort at Brezovica for an off-beat post-Yugoslav side trip.
trip.stayPick: Backpacker cluster (Ura & Bushati), old-town edge. The social centre of Prizren's small scene, walkable to everything, and the easiest place to find Sharr hiking partners. Marash is the shaded riverside fallback if you want quiet over social.

hoods.d: The tight social hub for the 25–35 overland crowd — hostel common rooms and nearby cafés. Plug in here to meet people.
Shaded riverside under a 400-year plane tree, a few degrees cooler than the old town. Café tables in real shade — your heat-smart work spot.
Modern, air-conditioned, locals-only zone where Prizren actually hangs out. AC and fast WiFi for serious work over 30°C.
Silent cobbled hillside alleys, the unrestored soul of the town. No cafés — an atmosphere walk at dawn or dusk.

eat.hook: The locals' no-nonsense best qebapa — voted over the famous square spots.
Tradition-meets-hipster — a coffee and a cheap meal, laptop-friendly.
Fresh burek or pite straight from the oven — the ultra-budget local breakfast.
Open since 1956 — roasted nuts, dates and Albanian sweets for pocket change.
Buy fresh mountain cheese and rustic bread for your glacial-lake picnic.
Heavy traditional food in a shaded riverside garden — cool and deeply local.
An old traditional inn with a family-run courtyard and hearty Kosovar comfort food.
Warming mountain-village food in crisp alpine air after the hike.

avoid.hook: Locals say it 'got greedy' and now targets one-time tourists.
A square tourist qebaptore trading on footfall — use Qebaptore Bekimi instead.
Guidebook-famous but locals say it coasts on its location and past fame.
A big premium for the view, and small tourist portions.
An overpriced commercial chain on prime real estate — the opposite of the cheap local teahouses.
A mall franchise — use the independent grills instead.

mobility.verdict: Walk the town; take the local minibus to the mountains.
mobility.cost: ~€0 in town + a few € round trip to Prevallë
mobility.note: The old town, Marash, Nënkalaja and Abi Çarshia are all on foot. For the Sharr day, catch the ultra-cheap minibus from the Marash bridge (departures around 7, 8 and 10am), or negotiate a shared taxi to a trailhead. Confirm the return departure on the morning bus so you aren't stranded up the mountain.

timing.verdict: Acceptable, with the same heat caveat — the town bowl can top 30°C.
timing.note: Split between dawn-and-dusk town time and a full cool day in the Sharr. Very good if you give the mountains a full day and keep the town to the cooler hours.

trip.name: Ohrid

currency.note: North Macedonia uses the denar. Cash-first — ATMs in town, fewer card terminals outside the centre.

trip.start: The only mass-tourism resort on the trip — so go anti-strip. Base in the quiet upper old town and spend days out: up in Galičica and down the quiet southern shore, off the boardwalk.
trip.outLeg: Long haul to Sofia (full travel day)

do.hook: The signature day — a gentle ~16km loop to a near-deserted mountain village through wildflower valleys, cool air, zero crowds.
The secret sunset spot up the dirt track, skipping the mobbed Kaneo boardwalk.
The easy southern day — a cold-spring swim off the unpretentious military beach, dodging the tourist restaurants.
A rugged 3.5km shore walk to a secluded church and isolated beach — the active alternative to a paid boat tour.
A tiny shaded wild beach a short walk from the centre — a cool-off swim away from the hotel strips.
Traditional mountainside life and a cheap beer at the village shop with residents; pairs with the hike.
Find the passionate local guide woman instead of a paid tour — the real cultural intro.
Quiet steps down to cheap street food and a genuine old-town the package tourists miss.

trip.doNote: Harder option: Magaro peak (2,255m) is the full-day summit with views of both lakes — strenuous and exposed, so only with a dawn start. The gentle Gorno Konjsko loop is the better fit otherwise.
trip.stayPick: Varosh, upper old town — off-beat old-town feel above the tourist bottleneck. Sunny Lake Hostel is the genuine, non-party backpacker hub: stay there or just drop in for cheap beers and to split a taxi up to the Galičica trailheads. Mesokastro is the quieter fallback with a balcony room over the lake.

hoods.d: Cobbled upper streets that stay quiet while the church squares below fill with tour groups — still the real old town, downhill to cafés.
Steep authentic residential steps above the crush, with sweeping lake views. Few cafés, but a cheap balcony room is ideal for deep work.
Traditional mountainside villages at cooler altitude — the gateway to the national park. A cool village day-trip base.
Quieter villages and beaches down the lake, away from the central crowds. Swim-and-eat day-trip territory.

eat.hook: Unpretentious old-school kafana — cheap grilled meats and cold beer.
The locals' best burek and banichki, off the tourist zone.
A slow-baked regional pastry straight from the market stalls.
Tomatoes, goat cheese and ajvar — assemble a cheap local feast at the hostel.
Tavče gravče and Belvica fish in a stone building — honest prices without the waterfront markup.
Fairly priced fish and beef stew, set well back from the tourist beach.
Fresh endemic Belvica trout on the water after your swim-and-hike day.
Traditional food and sunset lake views from cooler altitude — the mountain-day dinner.

trip.doNote2: Worth-it add-ons: Antep Baklava (cheap sweets across from Vkusno), the fish stew at Biser in Lagadin on the way south, and the smaller Kanevče for a cheap salad and rakija below Kaneo.

avoid.hook: Visually stunning but Western-European prices for what locals call disappointing food.
You pay a premium for the view, not the cooking.
Overpriced cruises — haggle hard or take the cheap public boat instead.
Tourist-trap dining right at the complex — walk to the military beach instead.
Genuine pearls come from only a couple of local families, so most cheap 'pearls' on offer are fakes.

mobility.verdict: Walk the town; cheap local buses and a shared taxi for day trips.
mobility.cost: ~€0 in town; €1–2 each way on lakeshore buses; a few € for a shared taxi up the mountain
mobility.note: The old town is compact but steep — bring good shoes. For the southern lakeshore (Trpejca, Lagadin, St. Naum) take the cheap local buses south. For Galičica and Elshani the trailheads aren't bus-served — split a shared taxi or hitch, which is exactly what the Sunny Lake hostel crowd organises.

timing.verdict: Good. The coolest stop at ~26°C thanks to the lake and altitude — no heat flag.
timing.note: Peak season on the lakefront, but the whole plan routes you off the strip into the mountains and quiet southern shore. The trip's breathing space — as long as you stay off the central boardwalk.

trip.name: Sofia

currency.note: Bulgaria uses the lev (pegged to the euro). Cards in the centre; cash for small spots and the metro.

trip.start: A thin closer, not a full stop — one evening and a short morning. A local base, a good cheap dinner, a place to meet people, an authentic morning. No day trips; Vitosha is out, there's no time.
trip.inLeg: Long bus from Ohrid via Skopje — arrive evening

do.hook: The memorable dinner — a cheap, off-beat hidden gem run by Freddie, who treats everyone like a houseguest.
The oldest open-air market and immigrant quarter — gritty, authentic, cheap street food, partly shaded. The best way to see real Sofia early before the heat.
Underground dive bar open until 7am, alternative and backpacker crowd — the spot to meet people.
The ultra-budget social move — alternative youth gather on the benches with cheap corner-shop beer and music.
The no-frills cheap-traditional fallback — rock-bottom prices, local kebapche.
A cool indoor bakery for a quality banitsa by your base before the airport run.

trip.stayPick: Oborishte & the Doctor's Garden, central Sofia — a quiet, historic, tree-shaded residential pocket local youth favour. Safe late at night and a short walk from everything, while dodging the tacky Vitosha Boulevard tourist strip. If you want something more social, stay near ulitsa Tsar Shishman.

avoid.hook: Scammers run 'OK Taxi' to mimic the real OK Supertrans and jump the fare 3–5×. Never hail off the street — use the Yellow or TaxiMe apps.
An overpriced tourist chain locals pan — skip it.
The strip your base deliberately avoids — overpriced for what it is.

mobility.verdict: Walk the centre; take the metro to the airport.
mobility.note: Central Sofia is walkable. Metro Line 1 runs to the airport — cheap and traffic-proof — but it serves Terminal 2. If you fly from Terminal 1, take the free inter-terminal shuttle bus from Terminal 2, so check which terminal your flight leaves from first. For a taxi, book through TaxiMe rather than hailing on the street.

timing.verdict: A thin closer by design — one evening and a short morning before the airport.
timing.note: No day trips. The plan does exactly what the window allows: dinner, a place to meet people, and a real Sofia morning before you fly.

legs.ds: Several daily; book ahead in summer. Flixbus / Gea Tours operate this.
Often via the southern route — a long travel day, the overland Serbia-into-Kosovo leg.
legs.warn: Fine on this route because you exit Kosovo onward into North Macedonia, not back into Serbia — do not backtrack into Serbia from Kosovo, which doesn't recognise Kosovo entry stamps. An overnight option may exist.

legs.ds: Usually via Tetovo and Skopje, or the Albania side. Fewer direct services, so check connections.
Almost always with a change in Skopje (Ohrid → Skopje, then Skopje → Sofia). Plan it as a full travel day; you arrive Sofia in the evening. Confirm both legs before the day.
Your flights out. Metro Line 1 to the airport from central Sofia.
